title: Refuting Dana Hall McCain's Nonsensical Anti-Abolitionist Slander
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds point-by-point to SBC Resolutions Committee member Dana Hall McCain's al.com article opposing the 2021 Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion. He argues that her six objections rest on judicial supremacy, sentimental loyalty to incrementalism, and a misrepresentation of abolitionist positions on equal protection.
audiences: Southern Baptist messengers and pastors weighing the 2021 Resolution
Pro-life advocates committed to incremental, court-driven strategy
Abolitionist activists training to answer common objections
Christians wrestling with judicial supremacy and the role of the Supreme Court
Curious onlookers asking whether women would be 'prosecuted' under abolition bills

questions.question: Why does Silberman believe McCain's framing of the Hyde Amendment as 'the most urgent front' in the abortion fight reveals a foundational error of pro-lifeism?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocates committed to incremental, court-driven strategy
questions.answer: Because focusing on a funding statute keeps the public debate off the central moral claim that abortion is murder. As long as Christians let the conversation be defined by ultrasounds, pain-capability, regulations, or taxpayer funding, the pro-abortion side controls the terms and wins, and abortion will never actually be abolished.
questions.quote: It is difficult to better encapsulate the foundational flaw of pro-lifeism than Hall has here. Someone who believes a funding statute is “the most urgent front” of the war over child sacrifice does not have their eye on the ball. We will never abolish abortion unless we are making the argument that abortion is murder and must be abolished.

questions.question: How does Silberman answer McCain's insinuation that messengers approved the resolution without reading it?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist messengers and pastors weighing the 2021 Resolution
questions.answer: He points out that abolitionists distributed thousands of pamphlets containing the full resolution and an FAQ that addressed the very objections McCain raises. McCain's complaint is self-contradictory: she charges messengers with being uninformed while simultaneously faulting abolitionists for the 'aggressive sidewalk campaigning' that informed them.
questions.quote: If we wanted to sneak the more controversial aspects of the resolution through undetected by the messengers, we would not have passed out more than 7,000 abolitionist pamphlets to messengers explaining exactly what we believe and why, with a specific emphasis on answering the hard questions.

questions.question: Why does Silberman reject the label 'fringe' that McCain applies to the abolition group?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists training to answer common objections
questions.answer: Because abolitionism is now the official position of the national SBC, Oklahoma Baptists, the Oklahoma Association of Free Will Baptists, and the Republican parties of Oklahoma, Texas, and Idaho. McCain herself ended up in the minority on the convention floor, so the old rhetorical move of dismissing abolitionists as marginal no longer holds.
questions.quote: Our position is the official position of the national SBC, Oklahoma Baptists, Oklahoma Association of Free Will Baptists, and the Republican parties of Oklahoma, Texas, and Idaho. The time of pro-life leaders being able to mock abolitionists as a small, fringe group is long past.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call Joe Pojman's 'force of gravity' comparison an act of idolatry?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with judicial supremacy and the role of the Supreme Court
questions.answer: Because equating obedience to the Supreme Court with an inviolable natural law subordinates God's authority to a human institution. A Christian's allegiance to God is reduced to a contingent partnership that only holds when the Court happens to agree with Scripture, which inverts the proper order of authority.
questions.quote: In equating obedience to the supreme court with a law of nature, Pojman is committing a form of idolatry. His allegiance to the true and living God is reduced to a temporary alliance whenever God’s Word happens to match the supreme court’s opinion.

questions.question: How does Silberman use a 'trot out the toddler' style hypothetical to expose the inconsistency of judicial supremacy?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with judicial supremacy and the role of the Supreme Court
questions.answer: He argues that no pro-life leader would tell states to comply if SCOTUS legalized killing toddlers or killing pro-life advocates, so refusing such submission for born humans while demanding it for the preborn betrays an arbitrary moral line. Since there is no morally relevant difference between born and preborn humans, the same defiance is owed.
questions.quote: If the supreme court ruled tomorrow that children up to age four can be murdered, none of these pro-life leaders would dream of telling the states to submit to that court opinion. Or to be a little more direct, if the supreme court opined that it was legal to kill people who oppose abortion, there would, of course, be no articles from pro-lifers demanding that we submit to that court opinion.

questions.question: What rhetorical inconsistency does Silberman identify between SBC Resolution #8 on the Uyghurs and pro-life resistance to immediate abolition?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist messengers and pastors weighing the 2021 Resolution
questions.answer: Resolution #8 demanded the Chinese government 'immediately' end its genocide of the Uyghurs and was passed without debate, but a parallel demand for immediate abolition of abortion is treated as fanatical. The pro-life logic, applied consistently to the Uyghur case, would absurdly call for merely defunding the genocide rather than ending it.
questions.quote: Everyone would have recognized the failure to be prophetic had someone tried to water down Resolution #8 by saying, “This is too uncompromising of a stand. We have to be willing to settle, and therefore, we should only demand that taxpayer money not be spent on the Uyghur genocide.”

questions.question: Does Silberman concede that incremental strategy has 'reduced abortion to almost nothing' in any state?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocates committed to incremental, court-driven strategy
questions.answer: No. He flatly denies the claim and labels it false. He argues there is no state in the union where the incrementalist approach has actually achieved that level of reduction.
questions.quote: Fifth, and very briefly, there is no State in the Union where abortion has been reduced to almost nothing. Very simply, this is a lie.

questions.question: Why does Silberman believe state legislatures already have what they need to pass abolition bills today, without waiting on Roe?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists training to answer common objections
questions.answer: Because in states like Oklahoma, Idaho, Indiana, and Missouri, pro-life politicians enjoy supermajorities sufficient to pass an abolition bill outright. The only obstacles are their own cowardice and theological and constitutional ignorance, often reinforced by pro-life leaders who keep urging them to incremental half-measures.
questions.quote: Pro-life politicians in states like Oklahoma, Idaho, Indiana, Missouri, and others have the bill to abolish abortion before them and the supermajorities necessary to pass the bill easily. There is nothing stopping them from doing so except themselves; their own cowardice and Biblical and Constitutional ignorance.

questions.question: Even granting the incrementalist's premises, why does Silberman think only an immediatist demand can ever pressure SCOTUS to overturn Roe?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocates committed to incremental, court-driven strategy
questions.answer: Because power yields only to demand, not to polite requests. Forty-eight years of incremental cases under largely Republican-appointed majorities have not rolled back a sentence of Roe; the Dobbs case will be the first time the Court hears a lawyer telling it it must overturn Roe outright.
questions.quote: As Frederick Douglass said, “Power concedes nothing without a demand. It never did and it never will.” It’s time to stop begging the supreme court for scraps. It’s time to start making demands.

questions.question: How does Silberman answer McCain's claim that the resolution mandates harsh, one-size-fits-all prosecution of women who have abortions?
questions.audience: Curious onlookers asking whether women would be 'prosecuted' under abolition bills
questions.answer: He says the resolution simply asks that preborn humans be treated equally under the law, meaning the same case-by-case discretion already used in homicide cases would apply. Women who are genuinely coerced would be immune; lesser pressure could yield lesser charges; each prosecution would turn on its own facts.
questions.quote: Just like for all other crimes, the circumstances of each case will determine whether and to what degree the individuals involved are prosecuted. Those who are legitimately coerced into murdering their child should be and will be immune from prosecution if an abolition bill passes.

questions.question: What three problems does Silberman attribute to the pro-life position that women must be automatically immune in all cases?
questions.audience: Curious onlookers asking whether women would be 'prosecuted' under abolition bills
questions.answer: It protects self-induced abortion from any legal accountability, removes any disincentive for women to kill their children, and is itself one of the historical reasons Roe v. Wade was even possible.
questions.quote: the pro-life, one-size-fits-all position that women should be immune from prosecution in all cases 1) protects self-induced abortion, 2) provides no incentive for women not to murder their children, and 3) is one of the reasons we got Roe in the first place.

questions.question: How does Silberman diagnose the 'pride in legacy' argument used by long-time pro-life leaders?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocates committed to incremental, court-driven strategy
questions.answer: He sees it not as a real argument but as a sin that blinds incrementalists to the merits of abolitionism. Decades of shared labor and personal identification with the pro-life strategy make any critique feel like a 'stab in the back,' so the position is defended emotionally rather than evaluated honestly.
questions.quote: But it is not an actual argument. It’s a sin that prevents many long-time incrementalists from being able to adequately assess abolitionism. It is perceived as a threat to their legacy, so they do not give it a fair hearing.

questions.question: What does Silberman identify as the most fundamental difference between the abolitionist and pro-life movements?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist messengers and pastors weighing the 2021 Resolution
questions.answer: The decisive difference is the source of authority each side appeals to. Abolitionist argumentation is grounded in Scripture, while pro-life argumentation rests on public opinion, bipartisanship, and science without ultimately being anchored in God's Word, which is why the first wave of rebuttals to the resolution contained no Scripture at all.
questions.quote: Pro-life argumentation, on the other hand, relies on public opinion and bipartisanship and science (none of which are inherently wrong to appeal to) without ultimately grounding their argumentation in God’s Word. This difference in what abolitionists appeal to as opposed to what pro-life incrementalists appeal to is a foundational difference between the two movements.

questions.question: Why does Silberman say McCain's emotional appeal at the SBC stage exemplifies a wider methodological problem?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with judicial supremacy and the role of the Supreme Court
questions.answer: Because while McCain wept appealing to her experiences at a Pregnancy Resource Center, she was simultaneously denying that abortion is sin requiring repentance and faith in Christ. The point is not that her tears were insincere but that emotional testimony substituted for the scriptural argument she could not produce.
questions.quote: While on stage at the SBC Annual Meeting arguing against the idea that abortion is sin on the part of the women murdering their children and that repentance and belief in Christ is the solution, Hall broke down crying and appealed to her experiences at a Pregnancy Resource Center. I have no reason to believe she wasn’t genuinely emotionally overcome in that moment, but I must note that making emotional appeals, not arguing from scripture, is Hall’s preferred method of persuasion.

related.title: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
related.reason: Companion piece from the same SBC 2021 cycle that frames the resolution and pre-answers many of McCain's objections.

related.title: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
related.reason: Direct news report on the convention vote that this article defends against McCain's al.com critique.

related.title: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
related.reason: Parallel rebuttal to other pro-life leaders Silberman cites by name (Klusendorf), continuing the immediatism-vs-incrementalism debate.

related.title: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
related.reason: Silberman's promised follow-up engaging the seminary professors and ethicists referenced in the Public Discourse article McCain echoes.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Lays out the abolitionist case against the very 'appalling' incremental bills McCain defends in her article.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the judicial-supremacy critique central to Silberman's reply to McCain's Dobbs and Roe-overturn arguments.

related.title: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
related.reason: The Dobbs amicus effort Silberman links to in the article as the first 'overturn Roe or else' argument before the Court.
